interpretation_summary: The preregistered replication tested whether allowing the possibility of losses in a threshold public-goods game reduces individual contributions, mirroring McCarter et al. (2010). The executors successfully rebuilt the original mixed-model analysis in Python, loaded the new 1,280-observation dataset, and fitted a linear mixed-effects model with random intercepts for participants and a session variance component. The plan–code alignment was high; only syntactic adjustments to statsmodels were required. While the original study reported a statistically significant reduction in contributions when losses were possible (F(1,61)=4.73, p<0.05), the replication found a negative but trivial and non-significant coefficient on the losses dummy (β = –0.545, SE = 0.929, p = 0.557). Thus, on the larger, independently collected sample the focal hypothesis was not supported.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: Full alignment: The executed code used the preregistered linear mixed-effects model (cont ~ losses) with random intercepts for participants and session-level variance, matching the stated plan. Data cleaning (list-wise deletion, none missing) conformed to the plan.

deviations.issue_description: Switched from statsmodels MixedLM initialisation to from_formula interface and created a lightweight wrapper script for container entry.
deviations.impact: Low: purely syntactic; did not alter model specification or results.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Individual contributions will be lower when the bonus distribution includes potential losses (losses = 1) than when it does not (losses = 0).
results_comparison.original_results: ANOVA contrast: participants contributed significantly less in the losses-and-gains condition; F(1,61)=4.73, p<0.05 (direction negative).
results_comparison.replication_results: Mixed-effects coefficient on losses: β = –0.545, SE = 0.929, 95% CI [–2.37, 1.28], z = –0.59, p = 0.557 (negative but non-significant).
results_comparison.overall_answer: No – the replication did not meet the preregistered criterion (β_losses < 0 and p<0.05); effect direction matched but was not statistically significant.

replication_report: Replication unsuccessful: although the sign of the effect matched the original (negative), its magnitude was small (≈0.5 points) and not statistically significant (p=0.56) in a much larger sample.

failure_handling.suggestions: Future replications could pre-test statsmodels syntax to avoid multiple edit cycles; include unit tests for wrapper scripts.
Consider additional robustness checks (e.g., round fixed effects, alternative clustering) to probe whether non-significance is robust.

notes: The replication used a broader participant pool (lab + Prolific) and four repeated rounds, which may influence behavioural variance. Nevertheless, the larger N boosts power, suggesting the original effect may be weaker or context-dependent. Sensitivity analyses separating first-round decisions or restricting to undergraduate sessions could be informative.
